Puzzle description:
HM-TRACE: Hindley-Milner type inference puzzle.

You must execute deterministic Algorithm W on a program and report:
- Principal type schemes for specific let-bindings
- The final result type
- The binding trace length M and specific bindings at checkpoint positions


OBJECT LANGUAGE:

Syntax (terms):
  e ::= x                        (variable)
      | (\x. e)                  (lambda abstraction)
      | (e e)                    (application)
      | let x = e in e           (let binding)
      | (e, e)                   (pair)
      | fst e                    (first projection)
      | snd e                    (second projection)
      | true | false             (boolean literals)
      | 0                        (natural zero)
      | succ e                   (successor)
      | isZero e                 (zero test)
      | if e then e else e       (conditional)

Syntax (types):
  τ ::= Bool                     (boolean type)
      | Nat                      (natural number type)
      | α_k                      (type variable with index k)
      | (τ -> τ)                 (function type)
      | (τ × τ)                  (product type)

Type schemes:
  σ ::= ∀ α_{{i1}} ... α_{{it}}. τ
Quantifiers bind distinct type variables; order matters for determinism.

Primitives in the initial environment Γ0:
  fst   : ∀a b. (a × b) -> a
  snd   : ∀a b. (a × b) -> b
  succ  : Nat -> Nat
  isZero: Nat -> Bool
  if    : ∀a. Bool -> a -> a -> a

IMPORTANT NOTE (about variable indices):
  Quantified variables in schemes (like a,b above) are placeholders and are NOT allocated from the global fresh-variable counter next_tv.
  Instantiation (event I) always allocates fresh α_k from next_tv and substitutes these placeholders simultaneously.

DETERMINISTIC ALGORITHM W (Damas-Milner) AND TRACE DEFINITION:

2.1 Fresh type-variable allocation (global counter):
Maintain a global counter next_tv starting at 0. A "fresh type var" is α_{{next_tv}} and increments next_tv.

Freshness events (and ONLY these events) allocate fresh type variables:
  (L) On entering a lambda \x. e, allocate a fresh α_k for the binder x before inferring the body.
  (A) On typing an application (e1 e2), allocate a fresh α_k as the result type before calling unification.
  (I) On instantiating a polymorphic scheme ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ, allocate fresh variables for each quantified variable in the order i1,...,it and substitute simultaneously.

No other fresh variables are permitted. next_tv is shared globally across the whole program.

2.2 Algorithm W (standard, fixed traversal order):

Type inference traverses syntax left-to-right.

W(Γ, x):
  look up σ = Γ(x); return (id, instantiate(σ)).

W(Γ, \x. e):
  allocate α_k via (L) for x
  compute (S, τ) = W(Γ[x ↦ α_k], e)
  return (S, S(α_k) -> τ)

W(Γ, e1 e2):
  (S1, τ1) = W(Γ, e1)
  (S2, τ2) = W(S1Γ, e2)
  allocate α_k via (A) as fresh r
  S3 = unify(S2(τ1), (τ2 -> r))
  return (S3∘S2∘S1, S3(r))

W(Γ, (e1,e2)):
  infer e1 then e2 left-to-right, composing substitutions as standard
  return product type of the inferred subtypes

W(Γ, fst e) and snd e:
  treat fst/snd as variables in Γ0, i.e. inference proceeds via lookup+instantiate+application; do not special-case.

W(Γ, succ e), isZero e, and if c then e1 else e2:
  treat succ/isZero/if as variables in Γ0 as well.
  You may desugar:
    succ e            ≡ (succ e)
    isZero e          ≡ (isZero e)
    if c then e1 else e2  ≡ (((if c) e1) e2)
  and then apply only the Var/App/Let/Lam/Pair rules above.

W(Γ, let x = e1 in e2):
  (S1, τ1) = W(Γ, e1)
  Γ1 = S1Γ
  σ  = generalise(Γ1, τ1)
  (S2, τ2) = W(Γ1[x ↦ σ], e2)
  return (S2∘S1, τ2)

Generalise(Γ, τ):
  quantify exactly the type variables free in τ but not free in Γ.
  Quantifier order is increasing numeric id of α_k.
  return ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ

Instantiate(∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ):
  allocate fresh β1..βt via (I), and substitute α_{{ij}} ↦ βj simultaneously.
  i1..it are exactly as stored in the scheme (which is increasing id under Generalise).

2.3 Unification algorithm (FIFO queue) and the recorded binding trace:

Use Robinson unification with a FIFO equation queue and occurs check. All unify calls made during W contribute to ONE global trace in temporal order.

unify(t, u):
  S = id
  Q = FIFO queue initialised with (t, u)
  while Q not empty:
    pop front (t1,u1)
    t1 := S(t1); u1 := S(u1)
    if t1 == u1: continue
    elif t1 is Var α_i:
      if α_i occurs in u1: FAIL
      else:
        extend S with binding α_i := u1
        RECORD this binding (α_i := u1) as the next element of the global binding trace
    elif u1 is Var α_i:
      if α_i occurs in t1: FAIL
      else:
        extend S with binding α_i := t1
        RECORD it
    elif t1 = Arr(a1,b1) and u1 = Arr(a2,b2):
      enqueue (a1,a2) then (b1,b2)
    elif t1 = Prod(a1,b1) and u1 = Prod(a2,b2):
      enqueue (a1,a2) then (b1,b2)
    else FAIL

Binding trace B:
  Over the entire W run on the whole program, concatenate the recorded bindings in the exact order they are recorded.
  Let M = |B| be the total number of recorded bindings (this is the "unification step count").

2.4 Prefix format for recorded bindings (certificate output format):

A binding is printed as:
  i=<decimal i>; <prefix(τ)>

Prefix type format is:
  Bool
  Nat
  Var <decimal k>
  Arr (<T1>) (<T2>)
  Prod (<T1>) (<T2>)

Parentheses are mandatory around Arr/Prod children and forbidden elsewhere except those.
Examples:
  i=7; Nat
  i=12; Arr (Var 3) (Prod (Nat) (Var 9))
  i=5; Prod (Arr (Var 1) (Var 2)) (Bool)

TYPE SCHEME NORMALIZATION (for Q1-Q3):

When reporting principal type schemes, use a normalised human format independent of internal α_k ids:

Scheme printing:
  If no quantifiers: print τ
  Else: print "forall a0 a1 ... . τ" with the listed a_j separated by single spaces.

Type printing (human format):
  Bool, Nat, a_j (for type variables)
  (t1 × t2) (for products, always parenthesized)
  t1 -> t2 (for arrows, right associative; parenthesise left argument if it is an arrow)

Normalisation of quantified vars:
  Take the scheme σ = ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ from the verifier.
  Replace these quantified vars by placeholders a0..a(t-1) in order of FIRST APPEARANCE when printing τ left-to-right.
  (So even if σ quantifies α_7,α_2, the renaming is by appearance in printed τ, not by id.)

The type of result (Q3) must be monomorphic (no forall).


Example:

Program (N = 3):
let t0 = (\x. x) in
let t1 = (\y. (t0 y, t0 true)) in
let t2 = (t1 0) in
t2

Execution trace (abbreviated):
- t0 = (\x. x): allocates α_0 for x, infers body type α_0, generalises to ∀α_0. α_0 -> α_0
- t1 = (\y. (t0 y, t0 true)):
  - allocates α_1 for y
  - instantiates t0 twice with fresh vars (α_2, α_3)
  - unifies and records bindings
  - generalises to ∀α_1. α_1 -> (α_1 × Bool)
- t2 = (t1 0): instantiates t1 with α_4, applies to 0, unifies
- result: (Nat × Bool)

Q1: Principal type scheme of t0: forall a0 . a0 -> a0
Q2: Principal type scheme of t1: forall a0 . a0 -> (a0 × Bool)
Q3: Type of result: (Nat × Bool)
Q4: M = 6
Q5: (example bindings)
  j=1: i=2; Var 1
  j=3: i=4; Bool
  j=6: i=7; Prod (Nat) (Bool)

Puzzle description:
HM-TRACE: Hindley-Milner type inference puzzle.

You must execute deterministic Algorithm W on a program and report:
- Principal type schemes for specific let-bindings
- The final result type
- The binding trace length M and specific bindings at checkpoint positions


OBJECT LANGUAGE:

Syntax (terms):
  e ::= x                        (variable)
      | (\x. e)                  (lambda abstraction)
      | (e e)                    (application)
      | let x = e in e           (let binding)
      | (e, e)                   (pair)
      | fst e                    (first projection)
      | snd e                    (second projection)
      | true | false             (boolean literals)
      | 0                        (natural zero)
      | succ e                   (successor)
      | isZero e                 (zero test)
      | if e then e else e       (conditional)

Syntax (types):
  τ ::= Bool                     (boolean type)
      | Nat                      (natural number type)
      | α_k                      (type variable with index k)
      | (τ -> τ)                 (function type)
      | (τ × τ)                  (product type)

Type schemes:
  σ ::= ∀ α_{{i1}} ... α_{{it}}. τ
Quantifiers bind distinct type variables; order matters for determinism.

Primitives in the initial environment Γ0:
  fst   : ∀a b. (a × b) -> a
  snd   : ∀a b. (a × b) -> b
  succ  : Nat -> Nat
  isZero: Nat -> Bool
  if    : ∀a. Bool -> a -> a -> a

IMPORTANT NOTE (about variable indices):
  Quantified variables in schemes (like a,b above) are placeholders and are NOT allocated from the global fresh-variable counter next_tv.
  Instantiation (event I) always allocates fresh α_k from next_tv and substitutes these placeholders simultaneously.

DETERMINISTIC ALGORITHM W (Damas-Milner) AND TRACE DEFINITION:

2.1 Fresh type-variable allocation (global counter):
Maintain a global counter next_tv starting at 0. A "fresh type var" is α_{{next_tv}} and increments next_tv.

Freshness events (and ONLY these events) allocate fresh type variables:
  (L) On entering a lambda \x. e, allocate a fresh α_k for the binder x before inferring the body.
  (A) On typing an application (e1 e2), allocate a fresh α_k as the result type before calling unification.
  (I) On instantiating a polymorphic scheme ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ, allocate fresh variables for each quantified variable in the order i1,...,it and substitute simultaneously.

No other fresh variables are permitted. next_tv is shared globally across the whole program.

2.2 Algorithm W (standard, fixed traversal order):

Type inference traverses syntax left-to-right.

W(Γ, x):
  look up σ = Γ(x); return (id, instantiate(σ)).

W(Γ, \x. e):
  allocate α_k via (L) for x
  compute (S, τ) = W(Γ[x ↦ α_k], e)
  return (S, S(α_k) -> τ)

W(Γ, e1 e2):
  (S1, τ1) = W(Γ, e1)
  (S2, τ2) = W(S1Γ, e2)
  allocate α_k via (A) as fresh r
  S3 = unify(S2(τ1), (τ2 -> r))
  return (S3∘S2∘S1, S3(r))

W(Γ, (e1,e2)):
  infer e1 then e2 left-to-right, composing substitutions as standard
  return product type of the inferred subtypes

W(Γ, fst e) and snd e:
  treat fst/snd as variables in Γ0, i.e. inference proceeds via lookup+instantiate+application; do not special-case.

W(Γ, succ e), isZero e, and if c then e1 else e2:
  treat succ/isZero/if as variables in Γ0 as well.
  You may desugar:
    succ e            ≡ (succ e)
    isZero e          ≡ (isZero e)
    if c then e1 else e2  ≡ (((if c) e1) e2)
  and then apply only the Var/App/Let/Lam/Pair rules above.

W(Γ, let x = e1 in e2):
  (S1, τ1) = W(Γ, e1)
  Γ1 = S1Γ
  σ  = generalise(Γ1, τ1)
  (S2, τ2) = W(Γ1[x ↦ σ], e2)
  return (S2∘S1, τ2)

Generalise(Γ, τ):
  quantify exactly the type variables free in τ but not free in Γ.
  Quantifier order is increasing numeric id of α_k.
  return ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ

Instantiate(∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ):
  allocate fresh β1..βt via (I), and substitute α_{{ij}} ↦ βj simultaneously.
  i1..it are exactly as stored in the scheme (which is increasing id under Generalise).

2.3 Unification algorithm (FIFO queue) and the recorded binding trace:

Use Robinson unification with a FIFO equation queue and occurs check. All unify calls made during W contribute to ONE global trace in temporal order.

unify(t, u):
  S = id
  Q = FIFO queue initialised with (t, u)
  while Q not empty:
    pop front (t1,u1)
    t1 := S(t1); u1 := S(u1)
    if t1 == u1: continue
    elif t1 is Var α_i:
      if α_i occurs in u1: FAIL
      else:
        extend S with binding α_i := u1
        RECORD this binding (α_i := u1) as the next element of the global binding trace
    elif u1 is Var α_i:
      if α_i occurs in t1: FAIL
      else:
        extend S with binding α_i := t1
        RECORD it
    elif t1 = Arr(a1,b1) and u1 = Arr(a2,b2):
      enqueue (a1,a2) then (b1,b2)
    elif t1 = Prod(a1,b1) and u1 = Prod(a2,b2):
      enqueue (a1,a2) then (b1,b2)
    else FAIL

Binding trace B:
  Over the entire W run on the whole program, concatenate the recorded bindings in the exact order they are recorded.
  Let M = |B| be the total number of recorded bindings (this is the "unification step count").

2.4 Prefix format for recorded bindings (certificate output format):

A binding is printed as:
  i=<decimal i>; <prefix(τ)>

Prefix type format is:
  Bool
  Nat
  Var <decimal k>
  Arr (<T1>) (<T2>)
  Prod (<T1>) (<T2>)

Parentheses are mandatory around Arr/Prod children and forbidden elsewhere except those.
Examples:
  i=7; Nat
  i=12; Arr (Var 3) (Prod (Nat) (Var 9))
  i=5; Prod (Arr (Var 1) (Var 2)) (Bool)

TYPE SCHEME NORMALIZATION (for Q1-Q3):

When reporting principal type schemes, use a normalised human format independent of internal α_k ids:

Scheme printing:
  If no quantifiers: print τ
  Else: print "forall a0 a1 ... . τ" with the listed a_j separated by single spaces.

Type printing (human format):
  Bool, Nat, a_j (for type variables)
  (t1 × t2) (for products, always parenthesized)
  t1 -> t2 (for arrows, right associative; parenthesise left argument if it is an arrow)

Normalisation of quantified vars:
  Take the scheme σ = ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ from the verifier.
  Replace these quantified vars by placeholders a0..a(t-1) in order of FIRST APPEARANCE when printing τ left-to-right.
  (So even if σ quantifies α_7,α_2, the renaming is by appearance in printed τ, not by id.)

The type of result (Q3) must be monomorphic (no forall).

- t0 = (\x. x): allocates α_0 for x, infers body type α_0, generalises to ∀α_0. α_0 -> α_0
- t1 = (\y. (t0 y, t0 true)):
  - allocates α_1 for y
  - instantiates t0 twice with fresh vars (α_2, α_3)
  - unifies and records bindings
  - generalises to ∀α_1. α_1 -> (α_1 × Bool)
- t2 = (t1 0): instantiates t1 with α_4, applies to 0, unifies
- result: (Nat × Bool)

Q1: Principal type scheme of t0: forall a0 . a0 -> a0
Q2: Principal type scheme of t1: forall a0 . a0 -> (a0 × Bool)
Q3: Type of result: (Nat × Bool)
Q4: M = 6

  j=3: i=4; Bool
  j=6: i=7; Prod (Nat) (Bool)

Puzzle description:
HM-TRACE: Hindley-Milner type inference puzzle.

You must execute deterministic Algorithm W on a program and report:
- Principal type schemes for specific let-bindings
- The final result type
- The binding trace length M and specific bindings at checkpoint positions


OBJECT LANGUAGE:

Syntax (terms):
  e ::= x                        (variable)
      | (\x. e)                  (lambda abstraction)
      | (e e)                    (application)
      | let x = e in e           (let binding)
      | (e, e)                   (pair)
      | fst e                    (first projection)
      | snd e                    (second projection)
      | true | false             (boolean literals)
      | 0                        (natural zero)
      | succ e                   (successor)
      | isZero e                 (zero test)
      | if e then e else e       (conditional)

Syntax (types):
  τ ::= Bool                     (boolean type)
      | Nat                      (natural number type)
      | α_k                      (type variable with index k)
      | (τ -> τ)                 (function type)
      | (τ × τ)                  (product type)

Type schemes:
  σ ::= ∀ α_{{i1}} ... α_{{it}}. τ
Quantifiers bind distinct type variables; order matters for determinism.

Primitives in the initial environment Γ0:
  fst   : ∀a b. (a × b) -> a
  snd   : ∀a b. (a × b) -> b
  succ  : Nat -> Nat
  isZero: Nat -> Bool
  if    : ∀a. Bool -> a -> a -> a

IMPORTANT NOTE (about variable indices):
  Quantified variables in schemes (like a,b above) are placeholders and are NOT allocated from the global fresh-variable counter next_tv.
  Instantiation (event I) always allocates fresh α_k from next_tv and substitutes these placeholders simultaneously.

DETERMINISTIC ALGORITHM W (Damas-Milner) AND TRACE DEFINITION:

2.1 Fresh type-variable allocation (global counter):
Maintain a global counter next_tv starting at 0. A "fresh type var" is α_{{next_tv}} and increments next_tv.

Freshness events (and ONLY these events) allocate fresh type variables:
  (L) On entering a lambda \x. e, allocate a fresh α_k for the binder x before inferring the body.
  (A) On typing an application (e1 e2), allocate a fresh α_k as the result type before calling unification.
  (I) On instantiating a polymorphic scheme ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ, allocate fresh variables for each quantified variable in the order i1,...,it and substitute simultaneously.

No other fresh variables are permitted. next_tv is shared globally across the whole program.

2.2 Algorithm W (standard, fixed traversal order):

Type inference traverses syntax left-to-right.

W(Γ, x):
  look up σ = Γ(x); return (id, instantiate(σ)).

W(Γ, \x. e):
  allocate α_k via (L) for x
  compute (S, τ) = W(Γ[x ↦ α_k], e)
  return (S, S(α_k) -> τ)

W(Γ, e1 e2):
  (S1, τ1) = W(Γ, e1)
  (S2, τ2) = W(S1Γ, e2)
  allocate α_k via (A) as fresh r
  S3 = unify(S2(τ1), (τ2 -> r))
  return (S3∘S2∘S1, S3(r))

W(Γ, (e1,e2)):
  infer e1 then e2 left-to-right, composing substitutions as standard
  return product type of the inferred subtypes

W(Γ, fst e) and snd e:
  treat fst/snd as variables in Γ0, i.e. inference proceeds via lookup+instantiate+application; do not special-case.

W(Γ, succ e), isZero e, and if c then e1 else e2:
  treat succ/isZero/if as variables in Γ0 as well.
  You may desugar:
    succ e            ≡ (succ e)
    isZero e          ≡ (isZero e)
    if c then e1 else e2  ≡ (((if c) e1) e2)
  and then apply only the Var/App/Let/Lam/Pair rules above.

W(Γ, let x = e1 in e2):
  (S1, τ1) = W(Γ, e1)
  Γ1 = S1Γ
  σ  = generalise(Γ1, τ1)
  (S2, τ2) = W(Γ1[x ↦ σ], e2)
  return (S2∘S1, τ2)

Generalise(Γ, τ):
  quantify exactly the type variables free in τ but not free in Γ.
  Quantifier order is increasing numeric id of α_k.
  return ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ

Instantiate(∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ):
  allocate fresh β1..βt via (I), and substitute α_{{ij}} ↦ βj simultaneously.
  i1..it are exactly as stored in the scheme (which is increasing id under Generalise).

2.3 Unification algorithm (FIFO queue) and the recorded binding trace:

Use Robinson unification with a FIFO equation queue and occurs check. All unify calls made during W contribute to ONE global trace in temporal order.

unify(t, u):
  S = id
  Q = FIFO queue initialised with (t, u)
  while Q not empty:
    pop front (t1,u1)
    t1 := S(t1); u1 := S(u1)
    if t1 == u1: continue
    elif t1 is Var α_i:
      if α_i occurs in u1: FAIL
      else:
        extend S with binding α_i := u1
        RECORD this binding (α_i := u1) as the next element of the global binding trace
    elif u1 is Var α_i:
      if α_i occurs in t1: FAIL
      else:
        extend S with binding α_i := t1
        RECORD it
    elif t1 = Arr(a1,b1) and u1 = Arr(a2,b2):
      enqueue (a1,a2) then (b1,b2)
    elif t1 = Prod(a1,b1) and u1 = Prod(a2,b2):
      enqueue (a1,a2) then (b1,b2)
    else FAIL

Binding trace B:
  Over the entire W run on the whole program, concatenate the recorded bindings in the exact order they are recorded.
  Let M = |B| be the total number of recorded bindings (this is the "unification step count").

2.4 Prefix format for recorded bindings (certificate output format):

A binding is printed as:
  i=<decimal i>; <prefix(τ)>

Prefix type format is:
  Bool
  Nat
  Var <decimal k>
  Arr (<T1>) (<T2>)
  Prod (<T1>) (<T2>)

Parentheses are mandatory around Arr/Prod children and forbidden elsewhere except those.
Examples:
  i=7; Nat
  i=12; Arr (Var 3) (Prod (Nat) (Var 9))
  i=5; Prod (Arr (Var 1) (Var 2)) (Bool)

TYPE SCHEME NORMALIZATION (for Q1-Q3):

When reporting principal type schemes, use a normalised human format independent of internal α_k ids:

Scheme printing:
  If no quantifiers: print τ
  Else: print "forall a0 a1 ... . τ" with the listed a_j separated by single spaces.

Type printing (human format):
  Bool, Nat, a_j (for type variables)
  (t1 × t2) (for products, always parenthesized)
  t1 -> t2 (for arrows, right associative; parenthesise left argument if it is an arrow)

Normalisation of quantified vars:
  Take the scheme σ = ∀α_{{i1}}...α_{{it}}. τ from the verifier.
  Replace these quantified vars by placeholders a0..a(t-1) in order of FIRST APPEARANCE when printing τ left-to-right.
  (So even if σ quantifies α_7,α_2, the renaming is by appearance in printed τ, not by id.)

The type of result (Q3) must be monomorphic (no forall).


Example:

Program (N = 3):
let t0 = (\x. x) in
let t1 = (\y. (t0 y, t0 true)) in
let t2 = (t1 0) in
t2

Execution trace (abbreviated):
- t0 = (\x. x): allocates α_0 for x, infers body type α_0, generalises to ∀α_0. α_0 -> α_0
- t1 = (\y. (t0 y, t0 true)):
  - allocates α_1 for y
  - instantiates t0 twice with fresh vars (α_2, α_3)
  - unifies and records bindings
  - generalises to ∀α_1. α_1 -> (α_1 × Bool)
- t2 = (t1 0): instantiates t1 with α_4, applies to 0, unifies
- result: (Nat × Bool)

Q1: Principal type scheme of t0: forall a0 . a0 -> a0
Q2: Principal type scheme of t1: forall a0 . a0 -> (a0 × Bool)
Q3: Type of result: (Nat × Bool)
Q4: M = 6
Q5: (example bindings)
  j=1: i=2; Var 1
  j=3: i=4; Bool
  j=6: i=7; Prod (Nat) (Bool)

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

# Merge-split between two ranks

    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Dense N x N matrix, block-row distributed:
#   each rank holds m = N / P consecutive rows
#   rank r owns global rows [r*m .. r*m + m-1]
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure bar(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    N = length(local[0])

    k = N - 1
    while k >= 0:
        (piv_row, piv_val) = global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k)

        if piv_row != k:
            swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, piv_row, k, N)

        barrier()

        piv_owner = row_owner(k, m)
        pivot_row = broadcast_row(local, rank, P, piv_owner, k, N)

        if abs(pivot_row[k]) <= 1e-8:
            barrier()
            k = k - 1
            continue

        r = 0
        while r < m:
            gi = rank * m + r                  # global row index
            if gi < k:                         # only rows above the pivot
                factor = trunc_toward_zero(local[r][k] / pivot_row[k])
                c = 0
                while c <= k:                  # touch only the leading (k+1) columns
                    local[r][c] = local[r][c] - factor * pivot_row[c]
                    c = c + 1
            r = r + 1

        barrier()
        k = k - 1

    return local


procedure global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k):
    m = length(local)
    # Local scan over eligible rows owned here (gi <= k)
    best_abs = -1
    best_idx = -1
    best_val = 0

    r = 0
    while r < m:
        gi = rank * m + r
        if gi <= k:
            v = local[r][k]
            a = abs(v)
            if a > best_abs or (a == best_abs and gi > best_idx):
                best_abs = a
                best_idx = gi
                best_val = v
        r = r + 1

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, (best_abs, best_idx, best_val))

        (p_abs, p_idx, p_val) = peer
        if p_abs > best_abs or (p_abs == best_abs and p_idx > best_idx):
            best_abs = p_abs
            best_idx = p_idx
            best_val = p_val

        j = j * 2

    return (best_idx, best_val)


procedure swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, p, q, N):
    if p == q: return

    m = length(local)
    op = row_owner(p, m)
    oq = row_owner(q, m)
    lp = p - op * m
    lq = q - oq * m

    if op == oq:
        if rank == op:
            tmp = local[lp]; local[lp] = local[lq]; local[lq] = tmp
        return

    if rank == op:
        peer_row = sendrecv(oq, local[lp])
        local[lp] = peer_row
    elif rank == oq:
        peer_row = sendrecv(op, local[lq])
        local[lq] = peer_row
    else:
        # Non-owners do nothing, but keep call sites symmetric
        noop = None


procedure broadcast_row(local, rank, P, owner, k, N):
    m = length(local)
    if rank == owner:
        lr = k - owner * m
        buf = copy_row(local[lr])         # send an owned copy
    else:
        buf = EMPTY_ROW()

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, buf)
        # If I don't have the row yet, take the peer's copy
        if is_empty(buf) and not is_empty(peer):
            buf = peer
        j = j * 2

    return buf


procedure row_owner(global_row, m):
    return floor(global_row / m)

procedure trunc_toward_zero(x):
    if x >= 0: return floor(x)
    else: return -floor(-x)

procedure copy_row(row):
    out = new list(length(row))
    i = 0
    while i < length(row):
        out[i] = row[i]; i = i + 1
    return out

procedure EMPTY_ROW():
    return new list(0)

procedure is_empty(x):
    return length(x) == 0
```

procedure broadcast_row(local, rank, P, owner, k, N):

        buf = copy_row(local[lr])         # send an owned copy

    j = 1

        if is_empty(buf) and not is_empty(peer):

procedure row_owner(global_row, m):

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P consecutive elements:
#   rank 0 holds a[0 .. m-1], ..., rank P-1 holds a[N-m .. N-1]

procedure compute(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

    block_total = 0
    for x in local: block_total = block_total + x

    i = m - 2
    while i >= 0:
        local[i] = local[i] + local[i + 1]
        i = i - 1

    right = suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P)

    i = 0
    while i < m:
        local[i] = local[i] + right
        i = i + 1

    barrier()
    return local



procedure suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P):
    partial = block_total
    right_sum = 0

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, partial)

        in_lower_half = ((rank & j) == 0)
        if in_lower_half:
            right_sum = right_sum + peer

        partial = partial + peer
        j = j * 2

    return right_sum
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P consecutive elements:
#   rank 0 holds a[0 .. m-1], ..., rank P-1 holds a[N-m .. N-1]

procedure compute(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

    block_total = 0
    for x in local: block_total = block_total + x

    i = m - 2
    while i >= 0:
        local[i] = local[i] + local[i + 1]
        i = i - 1

    right = suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P)

    i = 0
    while i < m:
        local[i] = local[i] + right
        i = i + 1

    barrier()
    return local



procedure suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P):
    partial = block_total
    right_sum = 0

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, partial)

        in_lower_half = ((rank & j) == 0)
        if in_lower_half:
            right_sum = right_sum + peer

        partial = partial + peer
        j = j * 2

    return right_sum
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P consecutive elements:
#   rank 0 holds a[0 .. m-1], ..., rank P-1 holds a[N-m .. N-1]

procedure compute(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

    block_total = 0
    for x in local: block_total = block_total + x

    i = m - 2
    while i >= 0:
        local[i] = local[i] + local[i + 1]
        i = i - 1

    right = suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P)

    i = 0
    while i < m:
        local[i] = local[i] + right
        i = i + 1

    barrier()
    return local



procedure suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P):
    partial = block_total
    right_sum = 0

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, partial)

        in_lower_half = ((rank & j) == 0)
        if in_lower_half:
            right_sum = right_sum + peer

        partial = partial + peer
        j = j * 2

    return right_sum
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Dense N x N matrix, block-row distributed:
#   each rank holds m = N / P consecutive rows
#   rank r owns global rows [r*m .. r*m + m-1]
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure bar(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    N = length(local[0])

    k = N - 1
    while k >= 0:
        (piv_row, piv_val) = global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k)

        if piv_row != k:
            swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, piv_row, k, N)

        barrier()

        piv_owner = row_owner(k, m)
        pivot_row = broadcast_row(local, rank, P, piv_owner, k, N)

        if abs(pivot_row[k]) <= 1e-8:
            barrier()
            k = k - 1
            continue

        r = 0
        while r < m:
            gi = rank * m + r                  # global row index
            if gi < k:                         # only rows above the pivot
                factor = trunc_toward_zero(local[r][k] / pivot_row[k])
                c = 0
                while c <= k:                  # touch only the leading (k+1) columns
                    local[r][c] = local[r][c] - factor * pivot_row[c]
                    c = c + 1
            r = r + 1

        barrier()
        k = k - 1

    return local


procedure global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k):
    m = length(local)
    # Local scan over eligible rows owned here (gi <= k)
    best_abs = -1
    best_idx = -1
    best_val = 0

    r = 0
    while r < m:
        gi = rank * m + r
        if gi <= k:
            v = local[r][k]
            a = abs(v)
            if a > best_abs or (a == best_abs and gi > best_idx):
                best_abs = a
                best_idx = gi
                best_val = v
        r = r + 1

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, (best_abs, best_idx, best_val))

        (p_abs, p_idx, p_val) = peer
        if p_abs > best_abs or (p_abs == best_abs and p_idx > best_idx):
            best_abs = p_abs
            best_idx = p_idx
            best_val = p_val

        j = j * 2

    return (best_idx, best_val)


procedure swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, p, q, N):
    if p == q: return

    m = length(local)
    op = row_owner(p, m)
    oq = row_owner(q, m)
    lp = p - op * m
    lq = q - oq * m

    if op == oq:
        if rank == op:
            tmp = local[lp]; local[lp] = local[lq]; local[lq] = tmp
        return

    if rank == op:
        peer_row = sendrecv(oq, local[lp])
        local[lp] = peer_row
    elif rank == oq:
        peer_row = sendrecv(op, local[lq])
        local[lq] = peer_row
    else:
        # Non-owners do nothing, but keep call sites symmetric
        noop = None


procedure broadcast_row(local, rank, P, owner, k, N):
    m = length(local)
    if rank == owner:
        lr = k - owner * m
        buf = copy_row(local[lr])         # send an owned copy
    else:
        buf = EMPTY_ROW()

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, buf)
        # If I don't have the row yet, take the peer's copy
        if is_empty(buf) and not is_empty(peer):
            buf = peer
        j = j * 2

    return buf


procedure row_owner(global_row, m):
    return floor(global_row / m)

procedure trunc_toward_zero(x):
    if x >= 0: return floor(x)
    else: return -floor(-x)

procedure copy_row(row):
    out = new list(length(row))
    i = 0
    while i < length(row):
        out[i] = row[i]; i = i + 1
    return out

procedure EMPTY_ROW():
    return new list(0)

procedure is_empty(x):
    return length(x) == 0
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P consecutive elements:
#   rank 0 holds a[0 .. m-1], ..., rank P-1 holds a[N-m .. N-1]

procedure compute(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

    block_total = 0
    for x in local: block_total = block_total + x

    i = m - 2
    while i >= 0:
        local[i] = local[i] + local[i + 1]
        i = i - 1

    right = suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P)

    i = 0
    while i < m:
        local[i] = local[i] + right
        i = i + 1

    barrier()
    return local



procedure suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P):
    partial = block_total
    right_sum = 0

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, partial)

        in_lower_half = ((rank & j) == 0)
        if in_lower_half:
            right_sum = right_sum + peer

        partial = partial + peer
        j = j * 2

    return right_sum
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P consecutive elements:
#   rank 0 holds a[0 .. m-1], ..., rank P-1 holds a[N-m .. N-1]

procedure compute(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

    block_total = 0
    for x in local: block_total = block_total + x

    i = m - 2
    while i >= 0:
        local[i] = local[i] + local[i + 1]
        i = i - 1

    right = suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P)

    i = 0
    while i < m:
        local[i] = local[i] + right
        i = i + 1

    barrier()
    return local



procedure suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P):
    partial = block_total
    right_sum = 0

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, partial)

        in_lower_half = ((rank & j) == 0)
        if in_lower_half:
            right_sum = right_sum + peer

        partial = partial + peer
        j = j * 2

    return right_sum
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P consecutive elements:
#   rank 0 holds a[0 .. m-1], ..., rank P-1 holds a[N-m .. N-1]

procedure compute(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)

    block_total = 0
    for x in local: block_total = block_total + x

    i = m - 2
    while i >= 0:
        local[i] = local[i] + local[i + 1]
        i = i - 1

    right = suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P)

    i = 0
    while i < m:
        local[i] = local[i] + right
        i = i + 1

    barrier()
    return local



procedure suffix_block_carry(block_total, rank, P):
    partial = block_total
    right_sum = 0

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, partial)

        in_lower_half = ((rank & j) == 0)
        if in_lower_half:
            right_sum = right_sum + peer

        partial = partial + peer
        j = j * 2

    return right_sum
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) == 0)
            i_am_low       = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            # optional but often used for clarity/safety with blocking comms:
            # barrier()

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Total N elements, each rank holds m = N / P elements
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure foo(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    sort(local, ascending=true)

    k = 2
    while k <= P:
        j = k / 2
        while j >= 1:
            partner = rank XOR j

            ascending_block = ((rank & k) != 0)
            i_am_low        = ((rank & j) == 0)

            keep_small = (ascending_block == i_am_low)

            local = compare_split(local, partner, keep_small)

            j = j / 2
        barrier()
        k = k * 2

    reverse(local)
    return local


# Merge-split between two ranks
procedure compare_split(local, partner, keep_small):
    peer = sendrecv(partner, local)
    merged = merge_sorted(local, peer)
    m = length(local)
    if keep_small:
        return merged[0 : m]
    else:
        return merged[length(merged) - m : length(merged)]


# Standard linear merge of two arrays
procedure merge_sorted(A, B):
    i = 0; j = 0; out = new list(length(A) + length(B))
    while i < length(A) and j < length(B):
        if A[i] <= B[j]:
            append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
        else:
            append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    while i < length(A): append(out, A[i]); i = i + 1
    while j < length(B): append(out, B[j]); j = j + 1
    return out
```

The following distributed algorithm is executed on a distributed-memory machine.

```
# Ranks are 0..P-1, P is a power of two
# Dense N x N matrix, block-row distributed:
#   each rank holds m = N / P consecutive rows
#   rank r owns global rows [r*m .. r*m + m-1]
# Local arrays are in memory and exchange uses sendrecv(partner, buf)

procedure bar(local, rank, P):
    m = length(local)
    N = length(local[0])

    k = N - 1
    while k >= 0:
        (piv_row, piv_val) = global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k)

        if piv_row != k:
            swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, piv_row, k, N)

        barrier()

        piv_owner = row_owner(k, m)
        pivot_row = broadcast_row(local, rank, P, piv_owner, k, N)

        if abs(pivot_row[k]) <= 1e-8:
            barrier()
            k = k - 1
            continue

        r = 0
        while r < m:
            gi = rank * m + r                  # global row index
            if gi < k:                         # only rows above the pivot
                factor = trunc_toward_zero(local[r][k] / pivot_row[k])
                c = 0
                while c <= k:                  # touch only the leading (k+1) columns
                    local[r][c] = local[r][c] - factor * pivot_row[c]
                    c = c + 1
            r = r + 1

        barrier()
        k = k - 1

    return local


procedure global_reverse_pivot(local, rank, P, k):
    m = length(local)
    # Local scan over eligible rows owned here (gi <= k)
    best_abs = -1
    best_idx = -1
    best_val = 0

    r = 0
    while r < m:
        gi = rank * m + r
        if gi <= k:
            v = local[r][k]
            a = abs(v)
            if a > best_abs or (a == best_abs and gi > best_idx):
                best_abs = a
                best_idx = gi
                best_val = v
        r = r + 1

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, (best_abs, best_idx, best_val))

        (p_abs, p_idx, p_val) = peer
        if p_abs > best_abs or (p_abs == best_abs and p_idx > best_idx):
            best_abs = p_abs
            best_idx = p_idx
            best_val = p_val

        j = j * 2

    return (best_idx, best_val)


procedure swap_rows_distributed(local, rank, P, p, q, N):
    if p == q: return

    m = length(local)
    op = row_owner(p, m)
    oq = row_owner(q, m)
    lp = p - op * m
    lq = q - oq * m

    if op == oq:
        if rank == op:
            tmp = local[lp]; local[lp] = local[lq]; local[lq] = tmp
        return

    if rank == op:
        peer_row = sendrecv(oq, local[lp])
        local[lp] = peer_row
    elif rank == oq:
        peer_row = sendrecv(op, local[lq])
        local[lq] = peer_row
    else:
        # Non-owners do nothing, but keep call sites symmetric
        noop = None


procedure broadcast_row(local, rank, P, owner, k, N):
    m = length(local)
    if rank == owner:
        lr = k - owner * m
        buf = copy_row(local[lr])         # send an owned copy
    else:
        buf = EMPTY_ROW()

    j = 1
    while j < P:
        partner = rank XOR j
        peer = sendrecv(partner, buf)
        # If I don't have the row yet, take the peer's copy
        if is_empty(buf) and not is_empty(peer):
            buf = peer
        j = j * 2

    return buf


procedure row_owner(global_row, m):
    return floor(global_row / m)

procedure trunc_toward_zero(x):
    if x >= 0: return floor(x)
    else: return -floor(-x)

procedure copy_row(row):
    out = new list(length(row))
    i = 0
    while i < length(row):
        out[i] = row[i]; i = i + 1
    return out

procedure EMPTY_ROW():
    return new list(0)

procedure is_empty(x):
    return length(x) == 0
```
